text: The patient is positioned laterally with the left side up. The surgeon begins mobilizing the descending colon and splenic flexure, ensuring not to go behind the kidney. The mobilization continues until the medial portions of the inferior and superior poles of the kidney are visible, exposing the hilum to locate the adrenal vein.

The surgeon uses two left hands and one right hand to mobilize structures superiorly, revealing the left crus of the diaphragm. There is minor bleeding from the plane, controlled using bipolar cautery. The spleen is flipped up, exposing the pancreas, splenic vein, and splenic artery. Careful dissection is performed to avoid bleeding, similar to medial to lateral colon mobilization. The medial portion of the kidney is visible and palpated.

The surgeon identifies the pancreas and adjusts the dissection plane. The splenic vein and artery are visible superiorly. Gyrotis fascia is opened at the hilum level, with the surgeon following the vein inferiorly while pushing the kidney upward to stretch the surrounding structures. The renal vein is identified, appearing more superficial than the deeper artery, and is followed inferiorly towards the IVC to locate the adrenal vein.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to remove fat surrounding the adrenal vein, opting for scissors for finer dissection of small vessels.

The surgeon clips and transects a structure, then proceeds to mobilize the adrenal gland from the kidney. The dissection is not yet complete, and the surgeon identifies the left crus of the diaphragm and the stomach in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to detach the adrenal gland from the kidney, ensuring no bleeding occurs during the separation. The kidney is visible, and the adrenal gland is carefully dissected away without blood loss.
The surgeon removes the fibrofatty layer from the kidney and diaphragm, ensuring no bleeding is visible, indicating correct plane adherence.
